title: Space Tech Briefing — May 29, 2026
summary:
Good morning, space enthusiasts! This is your daily dose of the cosmos, live from our studio, on this incredible Thursday, May 29, 2026. Get ready for a whirlwind tour of the latest in space technology, from groundbreaking educational outreach to a major setback for a heavy-hitter in the launch industry. Let's dive in!

First up, a truly inspiring story connecting our world with the International Space Station! Today, the French space agency CNES is hosting a fantastic live Q&A session. Two hundred lucky students are gathered at the CNES technical center in Toulouse, ready to fire off their questions directly to ESA's French astronaut, Sophie Adenot! She's currently orbiting high above us on the ISS, and this In-Flight Call is being broadcast live on the CNES YouTube channel. What an incredible opportunity for the next generation of space explorers!

And speaking of Sophie Adenot and education, she's also hard at work on a fascinating experiment called ChlorISS. Aboard the station, Sophie is actively growing plants as part of an educational project. But here's the best part: thousands of classrooms right here on Earth are replicating her experiment! It's a brilliant way to bring hands-on science and the wonders of space exploration directly into schools, inspiring young minds everywhere. You can even find tutorials to join in the fun!

Now, for some news that shook the space industry yesterday. Blue Origin faced a significant setback at Cape Canaveral, Florida. Their much-anticipated New Glenn rocket suffered a devastating explosion right on the launch pad during a static fire test. The incident occurred on Thursday, May 28, 2026. Thankfully, and this is crucial, there were no casualties reported. However, images reveal a gigantic fireball, a testament to the sheer violence of the explosion. This is now being described as the largest launch pad explosion to date in the United States. This test was a critical step in preparation for the NG-4 flight, which was slated for June 4, intending to carry 48 Amazon Leo satellites into orbit. Fortunately, the payloads were not yet installed on the launcher during the test. This incident will undoubtedly cause significant delays for Blue Origin's ambitious launch schedule.

That wraps up our morning briefing for today. From the inspiring heights of the ISS to the challenging realities of rocket development, space is always full of action. Thank you for joining us, and we'll be back tomorrow with more updates from the final frontier. Have a fantastic day, and keep looking up!
